title: Actions Authorisations
text: Navigation: Concepts > Actions Authorisations Actions Authorisations Flow Diagram The following diagram illustrates the states actions can flow through. Where actions do not go through an authorisation process, new actions will start their life in the "Outstanding" state and when the officer has finished the related work they will move the action to the "Completed" state. Where actions are to be authorised they will flow through additional states (in blue). Any action that is yet to be completed can be changed to an "Unable to be Completed" state by an administrator, and this state can be reversed via the un-complete process. Uncompleting and Unable to be Completed Uncompleting an action can be performed in the Minute Item form when an administrator accesses it via the Generate Actions screen. This places a completed action into the Outstanding state. Marking an action as "Unable to be Completed" can be performed in the same form. It can be done to any action that is not already in that state. Once in that state it can be reverted to the Outstanding state via the un-completing process. Actions Authorisations Process Activating Actions Authorisations To use actions authorisations first they must be activated: · In System Parameters-Actions: o Authorisations - Enable actions authorisations - Set to True to enable actions authorisations o Authorisations - Default actions authorisers from report - Set to True to automatically apply the report authorisers list to an action during the minutes upload process o Authorisations - Default original officer to first authoriser on reallocation - Set to True to the default the original officer as the first authoriser on reallocation. During an action reallocation the original officer will become the first authoriser for the new officer of the action. The purpose of this is to give the original officer some visibility to the action, specifically in the case where a supervisor might delegate the action to a subordinate. Uploading the Actions and Authorisations The process to create actions using actions authorisations is: · The minutes are completed at or after the meeting · The minute items are uploaded on the Generate Actions screen o For each minute item uploaded, actions will automatically be created. There may be one or more depending on the configuration on Agenda Items - Meetings and Section Tab or Procedural Motions screen. o Authorisers will be automatically added to items linked to reports (if the System Parameter above is on) o Use the Actions Tab on the Minute Item sub-screen to add, edit or delete authorisers. The Update Action Authorisers is invoked using the Authoriser button for the highlighted row. o Commit the actions to the officers. The Authorisation process on the Actions screen The process to authorise actions using actions authorisations is: · Once an action is finished the officer will notify the first authoriser and any delegates to the authoriser · The authoriser can click on the Waiting

title: Attachment Formats
text: Navigation: Concepts > Attachment Formats infocouncil can handle attachments in multiple formats and to do so it uses different processes. Word documents: Infocouncil will accept Word documents as attachments. In most organisations: The body of the Word document will be inserted into the compiled business paper but any headers and footers in the attachments will be replaced by those of the compiled business paper. In a few organisations: The header and footer in the original attachment will appear in the compiled business paper. Excel spreadsheets: Infocouncil will accept Excel spreadsheets as attachments. Only the active worksheet will be incorporated into the compiled business paper. Therefore, if you have a workbook with multiple worksheets to include: · Either save each worksheet as a separate workbook and attach individually to your Infocouncil report with its own title. · Or print them all to a single PDF and attach the PDF to your Infocouncil report with one title for the whole file. Check the layout of your Excel spreadsheet by going to File > Print and previewing it. PDFs and TIFFs: Word cannot incorporate PDFs or TIFFs as attachments. (That is a limitation of Word itself) However, Infocouncil runs a conversion process in the background that enables you to attach a PDF or TIFF file to a report that is in Word format. PowerPoint slides: Infocouncil cannot incorporate PowerPoint slides as attachments. Therefore you can: · Either, paste the slides into a Word document then attach that to your report. This is the preferred option. · Or, print the slides, scan to PDF then attach the PDF to your report. But remember that the quality of your attachment degrades each time a file is converted. Therefore this option is not recommended. Hard copy: Scan any hard copy items to PDF or multi-page TIFF. Save the soft copy file in an appropriate location before attaching to your report. Three-dimensional objects: Photograph the item, paste the photograph into a Word document then attach that to your report.

title: Attachment Sizing
text: Navigation: Concepts > Attachment Sizing Sizing an attachment is an important part of preparing it so the final document looks professional and is legible, and the agenda compilation process runs smoothly. Infocouncil can handle A4, A3 and other sized pages, but consider how they will appear if a user prints the attachment. Normally your attachment should be sized to A4 Portrait or Landscape or A3 Landscape; however Infocouncil will support other page sizes. When preparing an Excel spreadsheet you MUST set the page layout (go to Page Layout and set Margins, Orientation, Size and Print Area)

title: Cloud Report Editing Restrictions
text: Navigation: Concepts > Cloud Report Editing Restrictions Purpose Infocouncil has built in restrictions for editing reports in the Infocouncil Online application. The purpose of these restrictions is to allow a council to control which reports and reports types it's users are allowed to edit in an Online environment. A council will also have the ability to set a system parameter that would prevent editing of all reports online. A driving factor behind this functionality is the limitations of editing documents in Word Online. Word Online itself provides a subset of the functionality that is available in Word Desktop. Infocouncil report templates are designed to use the full functionality of Word. As a result there are fully featured report templates that will simply not work in an online word environment. To be able to use those report templates in Word Online that must be converted to a Word Online friendly format. The cloud report editing restrictions allow a progressive and controlled roll out of the converted report templates. Note that preventing report editing will still allow a user to view a report online in read only mode. Viewing a report is still subject to the rights a user has to access the report. The restrictions functionality provides the following features. · A System parameter to allow or prevent all report editing in the cloud · A report type (Agenda Item) flag that controls which report types may be edited in the cloud · A report flag that controls which reports may be edited in the cloud · A report flag that indicates whether a report was created online or on the desktop. System Parameter It is possible to prevent all editing and creation of reports in the cloud by setting the system parameter 'Infocouncil Online Reports Readonly' to false. This system parameter is available under the Reports section of the Infocouncil System Parameters maintenance screen. The default value for this parameter is false. It is advised that a council should only turn this system parameter to true when advised by Infocouncil Staff. Report Type Flag The purpose of this flag is to prevent the editing or creation of report types (Agenda Items) that do not yet have a Word online compatible template. Report editing and creation can be restricted by the report type that the report belongs to. The value for this flag can be changed in the Agenda Items Maintenance screen. The flag is entitled 'Cloud Editable' and the default value for all report types will be set to false. A false value for this flag will prevent report editing regardless of the value of the 'Infocouncil Online Reports Readonly' flag. Report Flag The purpose of this flag is to avoid the situation where a report created online but subsequently edited on desktop has edits added that are incompatible with Word Online. An individual report can be prevented from being edited online with this flag. This flag is controlled by the Infocouncil system and is not available to be set by a user within

title: Confidentiality Screen - Review Assistance Button
text: Navigation: Concepts > Confidentiality Screen - Review Assistance Button The confidentiality screen is able to have a button on it labelled "Review Assist". When clicked, this button opens a PDF document providing the user with some assistance with this screen. It was first created for Auckland Council together with the Review Date and Review Reason fields. The document was intended to provide assistance with how to set the review date specifically. Each closed act is allowed to have its own document. The Acts table stores the path to the documents. The button was only displayed if an act also required a review date or reason to be entered. The button's visibility was enhanced later on through a request by Wellington NZ Council to be able to display a general document about the selection of confidential reasons. A system parameter, under System - Display Assist Button On Closed Reasons, when true, forces the button to display all the time, regardless of act. An organisation using this feature needs to have their acts table updated so that when clicked the button has a valid document path to open. It is expected such organisations will use one common document regardless of the act selected and will put the same document path for all acts.

title: Configuring What Action Sheets Are Generated
text: Navigation: Concepts > Configuring What Action Sheets Are Generated During the minutes upload process, action sheets are automatically generated for each minute item based on what default actions have been set up. Default action sheets can be defined for reports, set items (procedural motions) or new items. After the minutes upload process the administrator can review, add to, edit or delete the automatically generated actions as desired. In considering what, if any, default action sheets to define, the aim is to minimise this add, edit and delete work as much as possible. For Reports From the Agenda Items screen, on the Meetings and Sections Tab you will see a list of Default Actions. For each Meeting that an Agenda Item is allowed in, default actions can be defined. During the minutes upload, each report will have actions automatically created based on these default actions. A default action is defined as follows Relates To The action can apply to the minute item or to each resolution for the minute item. Only one action is generated if the default action defined is to apply to the minute item. As many actions as there are resolutions for a minute item, will be generated if the default action defined is to apply to the minute item resolution. Action Officer Position The action must be allocated to one of the following positions and at the time of the minutes upload the relevant officer will be determined based on the position. If no officer can be determined, the action is not created. · Author 1: the reports first author · Author 2: the reports second author · Author 3: the reports third author · Author 4: the reports fourth author · Authorising Officer 1: the reports first authoriser · Authorising Officer 2: the reports second authoriser · Authorising Officer 3: the reports third authoriser · Authorising Officer 4: the reports fourth authoriser · Report Divisional Head: The head of the division related to the section the report is applied to · Author 1 Divisional Head: The reports first authors divisional head · Meeting Approver: The reports meetings default approver Default Action From Report If checked then generated actions will have the type and target date as entered by the author on the Report Data screen. If the Report Data screen for your organisation does not display an action type and target date, then the type will default to For Action and the target date will be determined by the meetings (committees) default action days setting. If the meetings default action days is not set then the date will be defaulted 14 days from the meeting date. Action Type Only applies when Default Action From Report is not checked. An action has a type of either For Action or For Information. Action Days From Meeting Only applies when Default Action From Report is not checked and Action Type is "For Action". If checked then generated actions will have a target completion date determined by adding the number of days in the meetings (committees) default

title: Custom Help
text: Navigation: Concepts > Custom Help Because Infocouncil is so configurable, either through the numerous parameters available in the system or through the custom scripting that occurs for each organisation, it is difficult to provide the exact help documentation desired by our clients. Custom Help provides a mechanism by which our clients may author their own help for none, some or all of the screens and also directly from the ribbon. This help can then be linked to Infocouncil via the maintenance screen. Infocouncil maintains a list of all points in the system where Custom Help can be linked to. Most of these points are screens. There are still some older technology screens which are gradually being migrated to newer technology which can not link to Custom Help. Besides screens, the ribbon can link to Custom Help. See section below on Ribbon Help Links. From the Custom Help maintenance screen the linking points can be searched for then modified to create the link. The help link can be to any file that the computer can automatically open based on its default program for the type of the file specified. Common types would be a pdf or an html file. NO WORD DOCUMENTS are allowed, they would not be able to be opened when many Infocouncil screens are open. Alternatively, the help link can be to an executable. For example hh.exe (the Windows help file CHM viewer). Then the arguments can be used to automatically navigate to a specific page in the CHM file. See note on using CHM files below. Ribbon Help Links There are a number of help links that can be defined for the ribbon. All of these are accessible by clicking the ? in the ribbon. Which help link opens depends: · There is a generic ribbon help link with the context Ribbon. · All other ribbon help links relate to an Infocouncil document type, e.g. Ribbon whilst Report Active: · When a document is active and there is a ribbon link defined for its type then the ? will be visible and when clicked will open the help link for that document type. · When no document is opened, a non-Infocouncil document is active, or when an Infocouncil document type for which no help link has been defined is active, then the generic help link will open (or if not defined the ? will not be visible). CHM File A CHM is a compile help format. It is a popular way to author help documentation. A CHM file can be linked to from Infocouncil. Specifying c:\Some.CHM will open Some.CHM at the start page. In order to link to a specific page do the following: · Enter HH.exe in the Help Link field of Infocouncil · Open the CHM file (outside of Infocouncil) · Navigate to the desired page · Right mouse click on the topic pane and select properties · Copy the Address (URL) into the Arguments field of Infocouncil · Now when you click the Test Link button in Infocouncil the CHM file will be opened at the specific page.

title: Deadline Dates
text: Navigation: Concepts > Deadline Dates When a meeting date is defined, it can have report deadlines associated with it automatically. These deadlines will be displayed in appropriate places such as when the report is opened, in authorisers' email notifications and in the Document Menu form. To configure deadlines some concepts need to be understood first. So we will start basic and rapidly work through the intricacies. A "report" is created for a "meeting date". The "meeting date" is for a "meeting" (e.g. "Council", "Planning Committee"). A "meeting" has a "meeting type". A "meeting type" is simply a categorisation of "meetings" where all "meetings" with the same "meeting type" will be associated with the same deadlines. A "meeting type" can have any number of "deadlines" defined for it. Each "deadline" has a "deadline type" which is simply a label to describe the deadline. When a deadline is displayed in Infocouncil it is referred to as the "xyz" deadline where xyz is the "deadline type" (e.g. there may be deadline types such as "Final Reports Required" or "Agenda Delivery"). A "deadline" is defined as so many business days prior to the "meeting date". A "deadline" can be marked as compulsory in which case it cannot be ignored when a "meeting date" is created, otherwise it may be removed altogether at the time a "meeting date" is created. A "deadline" can also allow a time as well as a date to be defined and the time can be represented as an actual time or as an informal time (e.g. "9:30am" or "Following the XYZ meeting"). The time for the "deadline" is captured at the time the "meeting date" is defined. A "deadline" can be assigned a "significance type". A "significance type" tells Infocouncil that this "deadline" is relevant to a certain event in Infocouncil. Such events are the email notifications to the various possible report authorisers. Having provided a conceptual overview we will now look at each component in the chain to setting up deadlines. Deadline Types One of the maintenance forms, Deadline Types , allows "deadline types" to be defined. As stated they are but a label for the deadline which Infocouncil uses when showing a deadline anywhere. Examples of deadline types and how they might be used will help to understand them. Example Deadline Types Example Purpose Reports Required for Draft Agenda The deadline by which reports should be largely ready. They may be unfinished but their form is indicative of what the final will look like. The deadline type may be intended to be used only for Council meetings where a draft agenda is prepared. Other meetings may not use this deadline type. 1st Authorisation The deadline by which all reports must be authorised by the first authoriser. Draft Agenda Creation The deadline by which the draft agenda must be created and delivered. Further changes are expected but in general they are minor in nature. The deadline type may be intended to be used only for Council meetings where a draft agenda is prep

title: Draft and Official EDRMS Locations
text: Navigation: Concepts > Draft and Official EDRMS Locations Show/Hide Hidden Text The Draft & Official Locations feature allows an organisation to separate areas in their EDRMS (Electronic Documents & Records Management System) for draft documents (writeable by all users) and official documents (read-only to almost all users). This allows for very controlled records management and prevents accidental document manipulation. Note: - This feature is only available to organisations who integrate Infocouncil with an EDRMS. In order to make use of this feature, it first needs to be enabled in the EDRMS section of System Parameters . When enabled, four things change in the Infocouncil user interface: · Extra Meeting (Committee) default EDRMS parameters are available. · Extra Meeting Date default EDRMS parameters are available. · Extra folder fields are available on the document Save form. · The Process Destination Folders function is enabled. The following provides more detail on each of these changes and how they affect the behaviour of the system: Meeting (Committee) EDRMS Defaults This screen is accessed via the EDRMS Parameters button on the Meeting Maintenance screen and allows for two sets of default EDRMS folders to be defined. One set for the current/working/draft folder/containers and one set for the official/destination folder/containers. Meeting Dates EDRMS Defaults This screen performs the same function as the Meeting (Committee) EDRMS Defaults screen above, except it is for data held at the Meeting Date level. It is accessed via the EDRMS button on the Meeting Date Maintenance screen and allows for two sets of default EDRMS folders to be defined - one set for the current/working/draft folder/containers and one set for the official/destination folder/containers. These locations do not have to be set unless the organisation does not want the Save form to fall back to the Meeting (Committee) default values defined above. Document Save screen This screen is slightly different for each EDRMS that Infocouncil integrates with. Please refer to the EDRMS-specific documentation appropriate to your organisation. The example pictured here is for an organisation integrated with InfoXpert and displays the extra fields for Destination text entry and browse button. Data entry is not usually required. Process Destination Folders function The Process Destination Folders function is accessible from the Functions drop menu in the Administration Group in the Infocouncil Ribbon. Choosing this option displays the following window. When you select a Meeting and Meeting Date then click OK, all papers and reports for the meeting are examined for a difference between the Draft/Current location and the Official/Destination location. If there is a difference, then the document is moved to the new location in the EDRMS and the database is updated. The majority of the window displays a log of all processing documents and the progress through the process. A value of Finishe

title: eVoting
text: Navigation: Concepts > eVoting Driving Factors eVoting is desired to be able to reduce voter influence. A voting member may currently withhold their vote whilst they see how other members vote and then vote based on this. eVoting should inhibit the ability to see how votes have been cast until the eVote closes. eVoting is desired to facilitate direct recording of a vote into the minutes by the voter instead of via the minute taker. This reduces the chances for minute taker error. eVoting is desired to more easily record the vote and any reason for the vote. If vote taking becomes easier then votes can be held on more motions providing transparency to the way members are feeling on issues. Casting votes with eVoting is desired to be able to be done from any device to avoid purchase, support and maintenance of specialist hardware. Casting votes with eVoting is desired to be able to be done from Bosch Dicentis hardware so that those councils already using this technology can continue to do so. This hardware does not collate voting results so Infocouncil will need to interface to this system to be able to initiate votes then collate the results back to the division tool then into the minutes. eVoting and manual voting should be able to be conducted together when not all voting members have eVoting functioning for them, e.g. lack of a device, malfunctioning device, network issues etc. Manual overriding/rectification by the minute taker must be available to cater for any technical malfunction or unforeseen issue with eVoting. Concepts eVoting: The functionality that facilitates the recording of votes electronically. eVoting can be turned on and off. When turned on, a web site to facilitate voting is automatically created, hosted and emails sent out to meeting members to allow them to connect to the site. eVote: A vote (division) held electronically on a single motion. Dicentis: Hardware produced by Bosch to facilitate voting. Consists of a controlling unit, to facilitate votes and one unit per voter for casting a vote. Up to 90 voting units can be connected. It has an API that Infocouncil can communicate with Overview eVoting will be able to be turned on whenever a minutes document is open. Whilst it is on, eVotes may be conducted via the Division Tool. The Division Tool will still be able to be used to record a vote manually. When eVoting is turned off no further eVotes may be conducted. Many councils project the minute takers screen onto a screen so that those attending the meeting can see where the minute taker is in the minutes document. As the minute taker will have the Division Tool open on their screen during an eVote, this will be visible to the meeting attendees. Two types of eVoting will be allowed in the system: Infocouncil or Dicentis. For Infocouncil eVoting: Voting members will receive an email with a link to a web site, which will be created on the fly when eVoting is turned on. The web site will be hosted locally not on the internet. Th

title: Export metadata
text: Navigation: Concepts > Export metadata Purpose Infocouncil Export functionality has the ability to produce metadata files for each report or paper exported. The purpose of the metadata is to provide a way for external systems to read the metadata and to know details about what has been exported. Uses for the metadata files include allowing a utility program to be written to automate pushing of reports and papers into an EDRMS that Infocouncil does not currently integrate with. Metadata files are json formatted. Location And Naming Metadata files will be created in the same location as the exported report or paper. Metadata file naming format is as follows info_metadata_<"report"|"paper">_<report id|paper id>.json e.g. info_metadata_report_3672.json info_metadata_paper_5273.json Metadata JSON formats The following tables identify each property that is being written for reports and papers. It also includes definitions of shared objects that are embedded in the report or paper objects. EDRMSNumber String Resolved Boolean Summary String EDRMSFinalFolder String MeetingDate MeetingSchedule Object Authors Author Object List Approvals Approval Object List Typist Author Object Attachments title: Keyboard Shortcuts
text: Navigation: Concepts > Keyboard Shortcuts Keyboard shortcuts occur throughout Infocouncil. Every user should learn and use at least some of them - even if you prefer using a mouse. Using them is easy and will save you time. Word keyboard shortcuts Always remember that Infocouncil is an Add-In to Word, so most of the shortcuts you probably know and use in Word will work equally well in Infocouncil. A few of the common ones are listed in the table below. Shortcut Description Ctrl+X Cut the selected text or object. Ctrl+C Copy the selected text or object. Ctrl+V Paste the selected text or object. Ctrl+Z Undo the last action. Ctrl+S Saves the document. Shift F9 Switch between a selected field code and its result. AltF9 Switch between all field codes and their results. F11 Go to the next field F7 Choose/Bring up the Thesaurus/Dictionary Infocouncil shortcuts In addition to Word's shortcuts, many shortcuts have been created within Infocouncil. Keep an eye open for them as you are moving around within the product. ALT+Underlined letters Many of the shortcuts are documented as underlined letters. Press the Alt key and the underlined letter. For example, you can click Alt+E to email the author and ALT+C to close the window. You can click on Alt + G to go to the item or Alt+E to go to the end of the recommendation. Letter beside Member's Name On a window that contains member's names, type the letter beside a Member's name. For example, you could type B then A then A again if Cr Greco proposed an alternative motion and Cr Fontana seconded it. Ctrl+ number on button When taking minutes you will find using the shortcut keys invaluable. You can click on Ctrl+1 to insert a resolution or Ctrl+ DownArrow to insert a new row below the current row. Documented in Tool-tip Title Read tool-tips because some of them document tool-tips.

title: Where Documents Are Saved
text: Navigation: Concepts > Where Documents Are Saved Infocouncil can save documents to a variety of locations. The option chosen by your organisation is therefore configured as the system is being set up. Some organisations use a file-based system, while others use an EDRMS such as HP Records Manager, InfoXpert, Objective or ECM. File-Based system In a file based system Infocouncil needs to be set up so it knows where to save the reports and papers to. This set up is done in the Meeting Maintenance screen (via the Access Database). For each meeting you click the paths tab and choose a path for each document type. You may also choose different paths for closed documents and non-closed documents. Where a document type does not have a closed path, closed documents will be saved to the non-closed path defined. At the time of saving the document will be automatically saved to the path defined and the user has no ability to specify an alternate path. EDRMS system For EDRMSs it is a little different. Default folders for saving documents can be defined for a meeting date or for a meeting or not at all. Default folders are defined via the EDRMS Parameters screen which is accessible from either: · the Meeting Date Maintenance screen, or · the Meeting (Committee) Maintenance screen. The parameters visible in this screen are identical, but one applies to the meeting date, the other to the meeting. The EDRMS Parameters screen invoked from the Maintenance ribbon menu shows a different set of parameters that do not relate to document locations. Each meeting date can have default folders set up for each of the document types. There is an additional default folder for confidential documents and an additional default folder as a default for papers. If the confidential folder is not defined then the system will default the save location to the document type folder. If the document type folder is not defined and the document is a paper of some kind then the system will default the save location the default paper folder. If the report or default paper folder is not defined then the system will default the save location to the default folders defined for the meeting (committee) instead. Default folders do not have to be set up for a meeting date. Each committee/meeting can have default folders set up. The possible default folders able to be set up match exactly what can be set up at the meeting date level. And the functionality behaves the same when a particular default folder is not defined. Default folders do not have to be set up at all. It is a common scenario to have default folders set up for papers but not for reports. Many organisations like to save their reports according to the subject of the report and so prefer not to have any default folder appear at the time of saving. At the time of saving Infocouncil will attempt to default the folder to save to. If no folder is determined because one has not been defined or Infocouncil has not set up correctly then the us

title: Meeting Events
text: Navigation: Concepts > Meeting Events The following is intended to provide some insight into what meeting events are all about, what is possible now and what the future will bring for them. About Meeting Events Meeting events are eventually intended to be recorded automatically by Infocouncil for many events types that occur in a meeting. We have had a few requests from our clients over the years that fit in neatly into this concept. One such request is to be able to report on member conflicts of interests. Another is to be able to report on attendance reporting. Another is to be able to report on which way members voted on certain items. We even had a request to be able to report on the minute items for which a certain member seconded over the year. When a conflict of interest is recorded in the minutes via the Insert Text tool for example, Infocouncil can record details of this event. When, who, what type of conflict and even a reference to the item or resolution the conflict was recorded in. These events can then be viewed via the Meeting Events screen, using the search criteria to refine your results, and then reported on. Currently Infocouncil allows Text Definitions defined in the Text Definitions screen to be flagged as meeting events. Conflict of interests are typically defined in Infocouncil via this screen. By flagging them as meeting events, when the Minutes Insert Text tool is used to insert a conflict of interest, a meeting event is automatically generated. Codes of Conduct are also defined as a text definition and it too can generate events if flagged as an event type, as can any text definition. Our vision is to gradually add more meeting events to allow for a variety of meeting reporting. Anticpated event types: · Meeting start time · Meeting suspension time · Meeting resumption time · Meeting end time · Attendance o Meeting member presence at the start of the meeting o Meeting apologies o Late arrivals o Left the meeting o Returned to the meeting · Division held o Individual member vote · Resolutions o Mover o Seconder · Minute item start · Minute item stop This information will allow us to meet the above requests and more. Many clients record their meeting audio and/or video. The set or subset of a meeting's events could make a valuable index allowing a media player to start the media at the exact time in the meeting of a chosen event listed in the index. Minute taking is very hectic and not all use the minute tools live in the meeting. Using them after the meeting means the date and time of the event will be incorrect. Further, the insert text tool might mistakenly be used. It is simple to delete the text from the Minutes but this does not delete the automatically generated event. The Meeting Events screen allows a user with the specific security right to add, modify and delete events, allowing these inconsistencies to be corrected. Meeting event types also have a category. The category allows like types to be grouped together.

title: Need for Ribbon Caching
text: Navigation: Concepts > The Infocouncil Ribbon - Startup, Shut Down and In Session > Need for Ribbon Caching Infocouncil caches the state of the ribbon between Word sessions. The state is what ribbon groups and tools are visible or invisible. It saves this information with application settings, the same location where form position and sizing and grid column ordering is stored. What a user sees on the ribbon is dependent on system parameters and user permissions which are located in the database. If the database is not available because, for example, a user has taken their laptop home and is offline, then the ribbon state cannot be determined. Why cant we just check if the database is there when Word loads and set our ribbon state according to whether it is available or not? When a database is not present it can take upwards of 30 seconds to determine the database is not present. Why this is the case is the subject of another topic. It is unacceptable for that user sitting at home to wait more than 30 seconds for Word to load whilst Infocouncil determines it is not functional. Particularly when it is most likely that the user has no need of Infocouncil when at home. Likewise if there is a network outage in the organization, Word should still be responsive even if Infocouncil is not. For this reason Infocouncil caches the ribbon state. When Word starts up the ribbon is restored to its previous state and Word is available with no delay from Infocouncil. It is only when a user first invokes an Infocouncil command, by clicking on a ribbon button, using an Infocouncil shortcut key, or clicking on an Infocouncil backstage command, that Infocouncil attempts to connect to the database. Should it fail the user will be in for a wait and will then see any errors. There are two caveats to the prior paragraph 1. If a user opens an Infocouncil document directly from an EDRMS or the file system and not via the Document Menu, then the documents will execute automatic open code which will need to connect to the database. 2. If a user uses DAS and has an ini file setting to open the DAS Menu on load of Word and loads Word directly not by double clicking on a Word document from Windows Explorer, then the database will need to be connected to. In these cases the user will experience delays and errors when the database is not available. Once it is established that the Infocouncil database can be connected to (and the Bridge template opens successfully and the client code in the ini file matches whats in the database) then the ribbon state is updated per the system parameters and user permissions. If it is established that Infocouncil has connectivity problems then the ribbon state is updated to only show the About Box and the Tools ribbon group. The About Box form is not allowed to have custom help (help authored by a client) because this requires database connectivity and we do not want to impact the loading of the form when Infocouncil is having connectivity problem

title: Opening the Bridge Template
text: Navigation: Concepts > The Infocouncil Ribbon - Startup, Shut Down and In Session > Opening the Bridge Template The Bridge template is not opened when Word starts up. It is opened when the user first invokes an Infocouncil command. Infocouncil gets the InstallPath key from the Infocouncil ini file and looks for the Infocouncil_Bridge.dot template there and opens it. It is this template which provides most of the connectivity between the ribbon and the rest of the application. If the ini file has no value for InstallPath and the file cannot be found then the following message will appear: "The Infocouncil.ini file had a blank InstallPath, so the Infocouncil_Bridge.dot could not be found. The Infocouncil Ribbon will not be able to function. Please see your administrator." If there is a path and the file cannot be found then the following message will appear: "Infocouncil_Bridge.dot does exist at the path - IniFile.InstallPath - the Infocouncil Ribbon will not be able to function. Please see your administrator." If any other error occurs in attempting to open the template then the following message will appear: "An error occurred trying to open IniFile.InstallPath/Infocouncil_Bridge.dot. The Infocouncil Ribbon will not be able to function. Please see your administrator." followed by any error message passed up by the system. See Also The Infocouncil Ribbon - Startup, Shut Down and In Session

title: Original PDF Attachments in Papers
text: Navigation: Concepts > Original PDF Attachments in Papers When a PDF version of a paper is created from the Word version, it is possible to include the original PDF attachments into the PDF paper. The result is · better quality attachment content in papers, particularly large-paged attachments, · attachment content is searchable, · attachment content is annotatable. A result which has not been achieved yet is that the attachment content is fully accessible. That is, a screen reader will not read out the attachment content, yet. A Recommended Approach Infocouncil strongly recommends for councils that display attachments within their papers that they adopt the following approach. 1. When creating the Word version of the paper, continue to include attachments but always use the lowest quality PDF Conversion setting. This is set per attachment to a report or the minutes, within the Attachment screen. Images of each attachment page will continue to be placed in the Word document. One option is to use the new Aspose converter with one conversion setting (e.g., Low;75) see the Inlined Image Converters section below. 2. This will produce a paper with inlined images in the fastest time and will still be a reviewable document, although the attachment content may still be blurry where large pages are shrunk to fit in the paper. 3. When converting the Word paper version to a PDF, configure the system to replace the inlined images with original PDF attachment page content. 4. This will produce a PDF paper with as good a quality as the original attachment. Attachment content will still appear within the margins of the paper page. Large pages will still be shrunk to fit but the attachment content, text, lines, shapes, pictures, etc, will be the same and can be zoomed in to see the fine detail. When printed the quality will only be as good as the dots per inch appearing on the printed page, but on the screen, quality will be as good as the original attachment. This is our current recommended approach to getting a reviewable and easy to change Word document version of a paper that when converted to PDF, gives maximum quality. A variation A variation to this approach that delivers a significantly more rapid paper creation process is to not include inlined images at all in the Word version. Configure the system to insert attachment page place-holders instead. The resulting Word document will insert a place-holder page into the paper for each attachment page. Each page will be sized (A4 or A3) and oriented (portrait or landscape) as though the inlined image was coming in. The time saving is achieved because there is no need to create an image from each attachment page nor import it into the Word document. The disadvantage to this approach is that attachment content is not visible in the Word version of the paper. However, when it is converted to PDF, the place-holders will be replaced with original PDF attachment page content. The final quality is the same as in the

title: Paper Conversion to PDF Choices
text: Navigation: Concepts > Paper Conversion to PDF Choices The following 3 choices are the possible ways in which Infocouncil can perform a PDF conversion. If any of these choices are not available to you, it is because a component may not be installed on your computer. The choices exist because PDF conversion has been achieved with different levels of success for different clients. A conversion choice that has worked for years may on occasion freeze with the introduction of a new Word or Acrobat version. Hopefully you will never need to change your choice of conversion. A single conversion approach would make life simpler for you until a conversion was not 100% successful for some reason. It is better to provide the means to attempt alternate approaches should the need arise and this screen explains the choices to you. All conversion processes consist of two steps, the actual conversion of the Word document to the PDF format, then the creation of the PDF outline to aid navigation in the PDF. The list of choices below are in order of the most recommended to the least recommended. The choice you make is remembered for the user for the machine. A different user, or using Infocouncil on a different machine will not be affected by the choice change. Convert with Word and outline with Infocouncil This choice is the recommended choice and should always be used unless an issue arises with a specific paper. No issues are known with this choice. Additional options with this choice are: · Publish the original attachments (use for web browsers) - when an attachment hyperlink is clicked an original version of the attachment (in PDF format) will be presented in a separate tab in the web browser · Embed the original attachments (use for LG Hub or similar applications) - the attachments will be embedded with the main PDF document (they will available from the paper clip icon in Acrobat Reader) · Optimise for minimum size - will reduce the size of the PDF. Image quality may be degraded. Convert with PDFMaker and outline with Infocouncil This choice will only be available if PDFMaker is installed on your computer. Avoid this choice if you experience the following: · Your computer freezes during conversion. Additional options with this choice are · Embed the original attachments · Optimise for minimum size Pre-outline with Word and convert manually. This choice is always available. This is a last resort where conversion is unsatisfactory by other means. A copy of the Word document will be created and placed in the publishing directory. It will be named, the original file name of the business paper with the file extension changed to TMP.DOC(X). A number will be added to the name if a file of the same name already exists. It is then up to you to convert this file outside of Infocouncil. Please note, this choice very occasionally produces a pagination issue due to the outlining method.

title: Publish Destinations
text: Navigation: Concepts > Publish Destinations Publishing involves the moving of HTML or PDF versions of Infocouncil papers to another location. HTML or PDF versions of the Word version (DOC/DOCX) of a paper must be generated before a publish operation can take place. It differs from Exporting (Archiving) which moves copies of the original, Word versions of papers, as well as report and attachments. Infocouncil currently supports the publishing via 5 methods 1. FTP 2. File Server 3. SharePoint 4. LG Hub 5. Web API Others may be supported in the future. Each destination method may have restrictions or peculiarities about it that don't apply to other methods. · FTP and Web API and File Server - Allows reference files to be published · FTP and Web API - Allows subscriber notifications to be generated · LG Hub - Prohibits HTML versions to be published Publishing via the FTP, Web API or File Server methods is normally to make papers available to a website, but not necessarily so. If the website is the Infocouncil Publish Website, then reference files should also be published to keep the web site updated with what meeting types and meeting dates are valid. Infocouncil allows unlimited publish destinations to be defined. Each destination has a type corresponding to one of the four publishing methods. Defining the destination involves 1. Filling out the configuration fields that Infocouncil makes available for the type, including imposing any restrictions on what the destination may publish, e.g. PDFs, HTMLs, Open and/or Closed (Confidential) papers, Draft papers. 2. Overriding this configuration at the meeting level, if a meeting should have a slightly different configuration, including different restrictions. The Publish window will automatically display a column for each destination defined. For the papers available to be published, it will respect which are permissible for each destination and block publishing of papers accordingly. See Also Publish - Screen Help Publish Destinations - Screen Help Meetings Publish Destinations - Screen Help Publishing Papers - How To Creating an HTML Document - How To Create a Publish Destination - How To Creating a PDF Document - How To Publish Destinations - Concept

title: Releasing Actions
text: Navigation: Concepts > Releasing Actions Releasing an action is point in time when an action becomes available on the Actions screen for the action officer to start doing work on it. Prior to this the action is only visible to the administrator via the Generate Actions screen. Whilst an action is not released the administrator has time to add notes, change the action officer, change the target date or remove the action entirely. An action can only be released once. Subsequent emailing, etc, does not release the action a second time. The release date time remains as it was. The act of releasing an action can occur · Automatically when the action is created during the minutes upload process · Automatically when the action is emailed, printed or committed to an EDRMS · When the administrator explicitly releases an action The Actions Visible on Upload parameter when set to True, controls whether actions are immediately visible after the minutes upload process. This might be desirable for your organisation. If your organisation uses Infocouncil to manage the actions through to their completion then we would recommend the Actions Visible on Upload parameter being set to false. This gives your administrator time to ensure they are in the desired state. Otherwise it is possible that an action officer could go into the Actions screen and work with an action that is yet to have a note added, a target date changed or worse, be allocated to a different staff member. Some organisations will want the actions released only once they have been emailed or committed to an EDRMS. Some will never email or commit to an EDRMS and will want to explicitly release the actions when ready. The Generate Actions screen (and the Minute Item screen) provides the ability to do this via the Process Actions button. Where there are unreleased actions this is made very visible in the Generate Actions screen.

title: Remote Minutes
text: Navigation: Concepts > Remote Minutes Show/Hide Hidden Text Using the Remote Minutes process allows you to disconnect from the network and work on the Minutes document(s) away from your office. This is ideal if your organisation ever takes its Council or Committee meetings away the main buildings and holds them in a community hall or other facility. Minutes documents can be taken offline (away from the network and database), updated remotely and then re-integrated back into the system. Multiple minutes documents can be taken offline. It is possible to override offline documents in the case where a laptop is lost, damaged or an unforeseen circumstance occurs that requires the documents to be no longer offline. Prerequisites The remote machine (laptop) MUST have SQL Express installed. We recommend that the version of SQL Express matches the version of SQL Server on the database server or is a more recent version of SQL Express (e.g. SQL Express 2008 R2 on the laptop can be used with SQL Server 2005 on the database server but SQL Express 2005 on the laptop cannot be used with SQL Server 2008 R2 on the database server). System Parameters The following system parameters must be set up:- Part of the process for going offline with minutes documents, involves Infocouncil making a backup of a cut down version of the online database. Once the backup is made it is then copied across to the remote machine. Knowing this helps to understand how the parameters are set. System Parameter Description SQL Server Backup Path Defines where the online database backup will be put but the path must be expressed relative to the database server. It is the local path on the SQL Server in which to store the backup. SQL Server Network Path Defines where the online database backup will be put but the path must be expressed relative to the Infocouncil users machine so is likely to be a share defined on the database server. It is the network path to use to access the backup. Changes to the .ini file The following section needs to be added to the Infocouncil.INI file. Please note this section MUST be the last section in the Infocouncil.INI file. [InfoCouncilRemote] DatabaseConnectionString=Server=.\SQLExpress;Database=InfoCouncilRemote;Trusted_Connection=Yes; DatabaseConnectionStringV6=Server=.\SQLExpress;Database=InfoCouncilRemote;Integrated Security=True;Trusted_Connection=Yes; ToolbarUserFolder=C:\InfoCouncilRemote Trace=No UseLDAP=No LogPath=C:\InfoCouncilRemote\Logs The DatabaseConnectionString(s) may be different in your environment. Server=.\SQLExpress is the default if you have newly installed SQL Express. We recommend that the database be named InfocouncilRemote but it is not necessary. ToolbarUserFolder must match the system parameter Remote Minutes -Working Path. LogPath should also match the system parameter Remote Minutes Working Path with \Logs appended. The following illustrate the processes: Going Offline The following illustrates the workflow for going offline.

title: Report Authorisations
text: Navigation: Concepts > Report Authorisations The following is intended to provide some insight into how authorisers are defaulted for a report and the authorisation workflow. How Authorisers are Defaulted Factors affecting what authorisers are defaulted include · the committee the report is for · the type of report · who the author is Currently up to four authorisers can be defaulted. Who those default authorisers are is determined in the meeting maintenance screen. Within that screen, the first through to the fourth default authorisers may be specified. A default authoriser is not specified by name but by a title or role of some kind. The possible titles are as follows · Author · Committee Approver · Divisional Head · Authorising Officer 1 · Authorising Officer 2 · Authorising Officer 3 · Authorising Officer 4 The titles are specified so that the names behind the title can change and the list of default authorisers automatically takes the change into account. The Author is the main author (or first author) of the report. It is an unusual selection to have the author authorising their own report but it has been requested by some councils in the past and persists as an option today. The Committee Approver is specified in the meeting maintenance screen, the "Default Approver" field. The Divisional Head is specified in the division maintenance screen, the "Division Head" field. The Authorising Officers (all 4 of them) can be specified for each author in the system via the author maintenance screen. On the authors screen, none, any or all 4 authorisers may be specified. For example it is possible to only enter the Authorising Officer 3 and Authorising Officer 4 but leave 1 and 2 blank. A committee may then specify Authorising Officer 3 and Authorising Officer 4 as 2 of the 4 possible authorising officers for reports for that committee. If the author of the report also happens to be the divisional head or the committee approver and that position is part of the default authorisers, there is nothing in the system that manages that situation. That title will still be defaulted as one of the authorisers. There are cases where certain organisations desire this behaviour and there are cases where organisations do not want this behaviour. If a title has no name associated with it, e.g. there is no committee approver or the authorising officer 3 is not populated for a given author, then that authoriser is simply skipped and not defaulted. Authorisation Workflow This section covers how and when authorisers are notified that their authorisation is required. It specifically describes whether all authorisers are notified at the same time or whether there is an authorisation chain where one authoriser is notified at a time. Possibly an organisation may combine the approaches in some way. After a report has been created and saved, the authorisers may be notified of their need to approve the report. A system parameter under Authorisation - Display the Notify All But

title: Security Options for Confidential Documents
text: Navigation: Concepts > Security Options for Confidential Documents If an organisation is using a file-based filing system then Infocouncil offers three methods of storing the documents in protected directories on a file server. They are: 1. Confidential folders based on Meeting and Document type . 2. Confidential folders based on Windows Login name . 3. Confidential folders based on Roles . Confidential folders based on Meeting and Document type Infocouncil documents can be automatically stored in a structure based on the type of meeting (committee) the document is to go to, and the type of document (report, agenda, minutes, attachment) being created. An example of this structure might be: In addition, folders for confidential documents could be created using the same structure: A single confidential folder could be created, or one per meeting type, or one per document type for each meeting type. Once the structure has been created two things need to done: 1. The Meeting Types (Committees) screen in Infocouncil needs to be updated. Click on the Functions ribbon menu and select Open Database . Go to System Maintenance > Meetings to open the Meeting window. For each Committee update the File Save Paths tab. Enter the save paths for Reports, Agendas, Minutes, Attachments and Action sheets. For each type of document a confidential path may also be entered (check the Closed flag). Directories should exist for each path. For example: 2. The IT department needs to apply security to the confidential folder(s). Note that: · Only the people that will have rights to create confidential reports should be given rights to the confidential folders. · Different groups of people can have rights to each confidential folder. · If a person attempts to save a confidential report to a folder they have no rights to, a message will prevent the save. · Administrators of Infocouncil should have rights to all the folders. Confidential folders based on Windows Login name Confidential documents may be stored in a directory with the same name as a Windows Login ID. Infocouncil will automatically save confidential documents to the logged in users login based folder. An example of this structure might be: One folder should be created for each report writer who has the rights to create confidential reports. To switch on the login based method, c lick on the Maintenance ribbon menu and select System Parameter > System node and set this flag to True . Then click OK : Once the structure has been created just one thing needs to done: 1. The IT department needs to apply security to the confidential folder(s). Note that: · Administrators of Infocouncil should have rights to the whole structure. · If the structure is based on a middle department level then certain people such as the head of department would have rights to all sub-directories to their department. · Typically, at Windows Login ID level, rights would be given to the person, their usual authorisers, and anyone else who can

title: Shifting Reports Between Meetings
text: Navigation: Concepts > Shifting Reports Between Meetings Infocouncil offers at least 4 ways to shift reports between meetings. Choosing which option to use depends on where in the meeting cycle you are, your user rights and what you are trying to achieve. The four methods are: · moving a report. · cloning a report. · deferring/referring a report. · transferring multiple reports. Moving a Report: Anyone with access rights to a report can move it at any stage up until it is compiled into an agenda. They may wish to do this because the report is not going to be finished in time to meet the agenda deadline dates, or perhaps it is decided that the agenda is very full and the report should move to the next meeting. Moving a report simply involves opening it, editing details in the Report Data Entry window and re-saving it. The report moves to the new meeting. See the Report Data section. Cloning a Report An author who needs to put identical or almost-identical reports to multiple meetings (e.g. all Local Boards) or to a committee on a regular basis (e.g. monthly financial update report) should clone a report. They can do this any time before or after the original report is compiled into an agenda. Cloning a report involves opening the original report, choosing to clone it, editing the cloned report (if you wish), saving the cloned report then closing both the original and the cloned reports. Cloning saves retyping the original report. The original report remains unchanged and in the original location. The cloned report is saved to the new meeting and can be edited as much as required. See the Clone a Report section. Deferring/Referring a Report A meeting administrator can defer or refer a report after the report has been put to the original meeting (i.e. agenda and minutes of original meeting have been completed). Deferring/Referring a report is used when the members of the original meeting resolve to defer/refer the report to another meeting. The item remains in the agenda and minutes, but the report itself moves to the new meeting. See the Defer/Refer section. Transferring Multiple Reports A meeting administrator can transfer multiple reports before compiling an agenda. Transferring reports is useful if several reports need to be moved. It saves opening each report, editing the Report Data, saving and closing it. The transferred reports move to the new meeting. See the Transfer Reports section.

title: The Infocouncil Ribbon - Startup, Shut Down and In Session
text: Navigation: Concepts > The Infocouncil Ribbon - Startup, Shut Down and In Session The Infocouncil Ribbon has to absolutely minimize its intrusion upon the operation of Word when its database is not available. If a user detaches their machine from the office network, say to do some work at home, Word should operate with no disruptions, delays and so forth. Should an Infocouncil function be invoked in this state then Infocouncil should provide a message detailing why it cannot operate. Infocouncil does not attempt to load the Bridge template nor connect to the database until an Infocouncil function is invoked. The follow provides understanding on how the ribbon functions. STARTUP The following sequence of steps occurs If Word version changes (including patches), preserve application settings between versions When the Word version changes even for minor patches our application settings file is left behind with the old version. If a version change is detected by Infocouncil, the file is copied to a new location related to the new version allowing our setting to continue to be accessible. Determine If Active Directory is used and user is in a valid group This accesses the Infocouncil ini file in order to determine this information. The ribbon is hidden for invalid active directory users. Ribbon state is restored to the last Word session where Infocouncil was successfully used The state of the ribbon (what groups and tools are visible) is attempted to be restored to the same state as when Word last shut down after successfully using Infocouncil. The Report, Agenda and Minutes ribbon groups are not managed here, they are managed whenever the active document in Word changes. When a new component is added to the ribbon, or the user is given access to an existing component they previously did not have access to, they will not immediately see the component until Infocouncil is first used within Word. Likewise when a component is removed from the ribbon, or the user has access to an existing component removed, they will still be able to see that component until Infocouncil is first used within Word. If the removed component is the one clicked, the user will see a message saying Sorry the xyz tool is no longer available to you. For new installations of Infocouncil there will be no save ribbon state. Instead just the Main and Tool groups will be displayed. Once Infocouncil is used the ribbon will refresh and hide and show all groups and tools allowed by that user. See Topic Need for Ribbon Caching The Ribbon name may be customized By default the ribbon name is Infocouncil. This can be overridden if the Infocouncil ini file key, RibbonCaption, has a value. For active directory users they may have RibbonCaption0, RibbonCaption1, etc keys for each group defined in the ini file. This will be used instead in this situation. If uses DAS may display the DAS Main Menu form immediately If Word was started directly and not by double clicking a Word document, then the In

title: User Security
text: Navigation: Concepts > User Security Most organisations will accept the default security that Infocouncil is initially provided with. This consists of the three security groups: Administrators, Division Users and Authors. Each user (author) added to Infocouncil must be assigned to a group. Each group has rights to a number of Infocouncil functions. There is much scope though to refine the security to exacting requirements. In order to take control of Infocouncil security some concepts need to be explained. A security function is one piece of functionality within Infocouncil. It may be as narrow as adding an action note or as broad as being able to access a particular screen. Each security function can explicitly be allowed for an individual or a set of users. Alternatively each security function can explicitly be disallowed for an individual or a set of users. A security group represents a categorisation of users to which users will be added to and to which security functions will be allowed or disallowed to. A security group may also belong to another security group. Members are added to a security group from the Author Maintenance screen. Members, as well as being people, can be other security groups. For example, the System Administration group can be a member of the Divisional Users group, which means the former inherits all the security rights of the latter. Groups can be added to other groups from the Security Group Maintenance form. Security functions are added to a security group via the Security Group Maintenance form. By default this grants access to the piece of functionality to members of the security group, it will appear with a tick next to it. Instead the security function can be added and the tick explicitly cleared which revokes access to the piece of functionality to the members of the group. Why don't we just not add the security function to the group in the first place? We could do this but it is often easier to add a group as a member to my group, thereby inheriting all its security rights and then if there are a couple of functions from the inherited group that my group should not be allowed access to, I can just explicitly revoke those two. In addition to assigning rights to security groups, rights can be assigned to individual users. The Security Function Maintenance form provides the means for this. The form, like the Security Groups form also allows groups to be assigned rights to security functions. A Security Function must be somewhere in the user's collection of groups and functions and enabled at least once for them to be able to use the function. If it is included and explicitly disabled even one time, then the user is blocked from that function. If the function is included and explicitly disabled anywhere in the collection, then the user is blocked from that function.

title: Who Gets What Authorisation Email
text: Navigation: Concepts > Who Gets What Authorisation Email The following topic focus's on report but the same applies to papers. When a report is ready the author will initiate the authorisation process by clicking the authorise button in the ribbon. The Authorisers screen will appear, listing all authorisers for the report. The first notification email The author will select the first authoriser and click the Notify button which causes an email to be addressed to the following: Authorisation Required Notification Email The first authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers Delegates is true Then any active delegates for the first authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers With Division Rights is true Then any active authors within the first authorisers division who have the access rights to approve documents The email will have its sender set to the current user of Infocouncil which is most likely to be the author but it could be the authors manager. The first authoriser approval The first authoriser or one of the delegates will click Authorise under the Authorise drop button in the ribbon which causes up to two emails to be sent. The first email notifies of the approval and is addressed to the following: Approval or Return Notification Email The current authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers Delegates is true Then any active delegates for the current authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers With Division Rights is true Then any active authors within the current authorisers division who have the access rights to approve documents The one who notified the first authoriser. This will most likely be the author but could be the authors manager. The email will have its sender set to the current user of Infocouncil which is most likely to be the first authoriser but it could be a delegate for the first authoriser. The second email is the notification to the next authoriser and is only sent if the first authoriser confirms to do this. The second email is addressed to the following: Authorisation Required Notification Email The next authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers Delegates is true Then any active delegates for the next authoriser If the System Parameter Authorisation Notify Authorisers With Division Rights is true Then any active authors within the next authorisers division who have the access rights to approve documents The email will have its sender set to the current user of Infocouncil which is most likely to be the first authoriser but it could be a delegate for the first authoriser. If the first authoriser returned If the first authoriser returned the report instead of approving it then the same Approval or Return Notification Email is sent, addressed to the same people but with a different message and subject. A second Authorisation Required Notification Email to notify the next authoriser is not sent. Any sub

title: Concepts
text: Navigation: »No topics above this level« Concepts Concepts provide deeper descriptions to explain the behaviour of Infocouncil. The topics here are not organised in any structured way. Many of the Screen Help and How-to topics will link to the Concept topics. Search by providing a few keywords and the word "concept" to locate the most suitable topic, if one exists. Alternately use table of contents to navigate to browse through the available topics.

title: Glossary
text: Navigation: »No topics above this level« Glossary Action Sheet An Action Sheet is a memo created by a meeting administrator and generated by the Infocouncil system. Most Action Sheets are distributed by email. Alternatively, your organisation may distribute paper copies. All action sheets will contain information about a decision made during the meeting. Some will just be for your information, but many will ask you to do something as a results of meeting's decision. Some actions will be very simple e.g. For filing. Others will be much more complex and result in the recipient creating a new report to another political meeting. Action Sheets that ask you to do something may have notes added to them. And ultimately they need to be finalised. Authorise (a report or paper) Approve. Business Paper A business paper is a document produced by Infocouncil that is most commonly an Agenda or Minutes but can also be an Attachments, Minute Item Attachments or other document. A business paper is generated by Infocouncil for a meeting and usually from the meeting's report and/or report attachments. Closed Confidential. Dicentis eVoting An electronic voting solution for minute takers, allow divisions to be held such that votes may be cast electronically via Bosch Dicentis hardware which then may be automatically entered into the minutes. Divisional rights Delegation / authority. EDRMS Electronic Document and Records Management System. HP Content Manager (TRIM), InfoXpert Software used by organisations for records management practices. Infocouncil eVoting An electronic voting solution for minute takers, allow divisions to be held such that votes may be cast electronically via any device that can run a browser which then may be automatically entered into the minutes. Papers An Abbreviated form of Business Papers. Return (a report or paper) Reject or decline.

title: How-to
text: Navigation: »No topics above this level« How-to How-to help provides step-by-step instructions on how to do a task in Infocouncil. It is organised into the following categories: · Retrieving an Infocouncil Document · Authoring and Authorising Reports · Printing a List of Reports for a Meeting · Creating Agendas · Creating a Minutes Document · Taking Minutes · Publishing Papers · Creating Action Sheets · Monitoring Action Sheets · Administering Infocouncil · Customising the Infocouncil Ribbon to Add Your Own Button Where appropriate, the how-to topics will link to: · the screen help topics that describe each screen, field and button in Infocouncil, and · other topics of a more "conceptual" nature. Click on the links above or navigate through the table of contents to get to the task for which you are seeking help.

title: Troubleshooting
text: Navigation: »No topics above this level« Troubleshooting Common problems and their resolutions can be found in this section.

title: Welcome
text: Navigation: »No topics above this level« Welcome What would you like help with? · An Infocouncil screen , · How-to do something in Infocouncil, · Understanding a concept in Infocouncil. The Infocouncil screen topics describe every field and button on every window and form. Each also explains how to get to the window, what you can do on the window or why you might want to use it. It is often a good place to start. The How-to topics briefly describe the steps to perform a task. They offer very little explanation, but contain many links back to the screen-help topics. The Concept topics provide deeper descriptions to explain the behaviour of Infocouncil. Check the Glossary topic if you incur a term you are not quite sure about. This help is for version 8.10. ******************** Infocouncil Reports Quick Reference Follow these steps to produce reports for a meeting: 1. To create a new report click on the button on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Main Group. See the Writing a Report topic. 2. To add attachments to a report, first open the report then click on the button on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Report Group. See the Adding Attachments topic. 3. To initiate the authorisation process , first open the report then click on the button on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Report Group. See the Sending a Report for Authorisation topic. 4. To retrieve a report for editing click on the buttons on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Main Group. See the Retrieving an Infocouncil Document topic. 5. To make a report confidential, first open the report then click on the on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Report Group. See the Confidential Reports topic. 6. To print details of all reports for a meeting, click on the button on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Main Group. See the Print a list of Reports for a Meeting topic. 7. To create a cover sheet or see the attachments for a report, first open the report then go to on the Infocouncil Ribbon in the Report Group. See the Previewing Attachments topic.

title: Adding Additional Action Sheets
text: Navigation: How-to > Generating Action Sheets and Uploading Minutes > Adding Additional Action Sheets 1. To add additional action sheets to a minute item, click on the Generate Actions button on the Infocouncil ribbon. The Generate Actions window opens. 2. Choose the Meeting , Meeting Date and Minutes Type of the minutes document containing the minute item. 3. If the minutes document has not yet been uploaded , do this before proceeding. 4. A list of minute items will appear. 5. Select an item then the Action Sheets button. The Minute Item window for the selected item opens to the Action Sheets tab. Double clicking opens the same window but to the Details tab. 6. The top half of the window opens any other action sheets that have already been created for the item (either automatically at the time of the upload or manually by an administrator). 7. Click the Add button underneath the list of Action Sheets (not the Add button underneath the list of notes). 8. The action sheet will automatically default its values which may be changed as desired. 9. To add action notes to an action sheet, select it in the top part of the window then click the Add button under the Action Notes list near the bottom of the window. The Action Note window opens. Add a note then click OK . 10. Click the Save button to save the Action Sheet and any notes you added. 11. Click Close to close the window. See Also Generate Actions Minute Item Action Note Working With an Individual Action Sheet - How To

title: Adding a Reference Type
text: Navigation: How-to > Administering Infocouncil > Maintaining Reference Types > Adding a Reference Type Adding a Reference Type would normally only be performed by Infocouncil staff. Presently items in a reference type are selected for certain reports which scripting then uses to render the selection into the final report. Adding a reference type without the supporting scripting means its item selections will not appear in reports. However sometime in the future Infocouncil will allow reporting on reference types. At this time, it may be fine not to render item selection into a report for some reference types. But having the ability to report on these reference types could be very useful. For this reason, the steps are listed. 1. Click on Reference Types under the Maintenance drop down, in the Administration group of the ribbon. The Reference Types Maintenance window opens. 2. Enter a unique label for the reference type. 3. Enter a version label for the reference type. On the Report Data form, when this type is selected the label for its versions will be changed to this. 4. Enter an item label for the reference type. On the Report Data form, when this type is selected the label for its list of items will be changed to this. 5. Go to the Report Types tab and select which Report Types will use this reference type. Once selected the Report Data screen will show this reference type for the user to select items from. 6. For each report type using the reference type a. Change the Reference Type Order as desired. You will see all reference types the selected report type uses, and you will be able to set the order as they will appear on the Report Data form here. b. Set what items can be selected for the reference type and report type c. Set whether selection is prohibited from the same lineage. If true, then if a user selects an item, then they will be prevented from additionally selecting one of the items ancestors (parent, grandparent, etc) or descendants (child, grandchild, etc). d. Set the agenda defaulting rules i. Set Reference type item selection defaults agenda section to true, if you want the agenda section to be defaulted on the Report Data form, when a reference type item is selected and either the item has a default agenda section specified, or the item has a report level specified which references another item which does have a default agenda section specified. ii. Set Agenda section selection defaults reference type item to true, if when the agenda section is selected on the Report Data form, you want any reference type items with a matching default agenda section, to be automatically selected. 7. Click Save 8. Now a version and its items can be added. See Adding a Reference Type Version - How To. 9. On finishing with the versions, order the versions on the Details tab as you wish them to appear on the Report Data screen in the Reference Type versions combo box. 10. Click Save See Also Reference Types - Screen Help Maintaining reference Typ

title: Adding a Reference Type Version
text: Navigation: How-to > Administering Infocouncil > Maintaining Reference Types > Adding a Reference Type Version 1. Click on Reference Types under the Maintenance drop down, in the Administration group of the ribbon. The Reference Types Maintenance window opens. 2.
